verses.commentary: Jesus praying in a certain place. When he finished, a disciple: 'Lord, teach us to pray, as John taught his disciples.' Discipleship-by-pattern requested.

'When you pray, say: Father, hallowed be your name. Your kingdom come.' Lukan version of the Lord's Prayer — shorter than Matthew's. Father (Pater, Πάτερ) directly — no 'our' or 'in heaven.' Intimate.
'Give us each day our daily bread.' Kath' hemeran (καθ' ἡμέραν) — each day. Manna economics — today's supply.
'Forgive us our sins, for we ourselves forgive everyone indebted to us. Lead us not into temptation.' Reciprocity in forgiveness. Final petition shorter.
Parable: a friend goes at midnight to a friend — 'Friend, lend me three loaves.'
'A friend of mine has arrived from a journey, and I have nothing to set before him.' Honor-shame culture: failure to feed a guest is dishonor.
From inside: 'Do not bother me. The door is shut, my children are with me in bed. I cannot rise and give to you.' Reasonable nighttime refusal.

'Though he will not rise because of friendship, yet because of his impudence (anaideia, ἀναίδεια — shameless persistence) he will rise and give him whatever he needs.' Persistence overcomes reluctance.
'Ask and it will be given; seek and you will find; knock and it will be opened.' Three present imperatives. Confident continual petition.
Universal scope: 'Everyone who asks receives.' Same as Matthew 7:8.
Father analogy: son asks for fish — gives a serpent?
Or asks for an egg — gives a scorpion? (Lukan additional pair.) Even worst parents do not betray basic needs.

If you who are evil know how to give good gifts to your children, how much more will the heavenly Father give the Holy Spirit (Lukan distinctive — Matthew has 'good gifts') to those who ask. The supreme gift is the Spirit.
Driving out a demon that was mute. When the demon left, the mute man spoke. Crowd marveled.
Some: 'By Beelzebul, the prince of demons, he casts out demons.' Same accusation as Matthew 12:24.
Others tested him, seeking from him a sign from heaven. Two-pronged opposition.
Knowing their thoughts: 'Every kingdom divided against itself is laid waste; a divided house falls.' Political wisdom recycled theologically.
If Satan is divided — how does his kingdom stand? The accusation collapses logically.
'If I cast out demons by Beelzebul, by whom do your sons cast them out? They will be your judges.' Same activity by their own people — they cannot have it both ways.

'If by the finger of God (daktylo theou, δακτύλῳ θεοῦ — Exodus 8:19 echo) I cast out demons, then the kingdom of God has come upon you.' Matthew has 'Spirit of God'; Luke 'finger.' Both phrases evoke Exodus. The exorcisms are kingdom-arrivals.
Strong man fully armed guards his own house — his goods are safe.
But when one stronger attacks him and overcomes him, he takes away his armor in which he trusted and divides his spoil. Christ as the stronger one binding Satan.
'Whoever is not with me is against me; whoever does not gather with me scatters.' Neutrality is impossible (compare 9:50 — different audience, different application).
When the unclean spirit has gone out — wanders waterless places seeking rest. Finds none.
Returns: finds the house swept clean and put in order. Empty.
Goes and brings seven other spirits more wicked than itself; they go in and dwell. The last state of that person is worse than the first. Reformation without regeneration backfires.
A woman in the crowd: 'Blessed is the womb that bore you and the breasts at which you nursed.' Maternal-blessing theme.
'Blessed rather are those who hear the word of God and keep it.' Redirects from biology to obedience. The greater blessing is on hearing-and-doing, not on physical relationship to him.
As crowds increased: 'This generation is an evil generation. It seeks for a sign, but no sign will be given to it except the sign of Jonah.'
'As Jonah was a sign to the Ninevites, so will the Son of Man be to this generation.' Jonah's preaching (not his fish-experience, in Luke's version) is the parallel.
Queen of the South will rise at the judgment and condemn this generation — she came to listen to Solomon's wisdom — and behold, something greater than Solomon is here.
Men of Nineveh will rise and condemn — they repented at Jonah's preaching — and behold, something greater than Jonah is here. Two Gentile precedents put Israelite unbelief to shame.
No one lights a lamp and puts it in a cellar or under a basket — puts it on a stand so those who enter may see the light. Hidden light is futile.
Your eye is the lamp of your body. Single (haplous, ἁπλοῦς — undivided, generous) eye fills body with light. Bad eye fills body with darkness. Vision shapes whole.
'Therefore be careful lest the light in you be darkness.' Self-examination required — interior light can be counterfeit.
If your whole body is full of light, having no part dark, it will be wholly bright, as when a lamp with its rays gives you light. Saturation of light is the goal.
Pharisee invites Jesus to dine. Jesus reclines at the table.
Pharisee surprised that Jesus did not first wash (ebaptisthe, ἐβαπτίσθη — ritual washing) before dinner. Tradition violation noticed.
'You Pharisees cleanse the outside of cup and dish, but inside you are full of greed (harpages, ἁρπαγῆς) and wickedness.' Outer-inner inversion.
'Fools! Did not he who made the outside make the inside also?' Creator made both. Cleansing only outside is incoherent.
'Give as alms what is within (or possibly: those things that are within) — and everything will be clean for you.' Alms-giving as purgative. Generosity flips the cleanness equation.
Woe to Pharisees — tithe mint, rue, every herb — neglect justice and the love of God. These ought to have been done without neglecting the others. Lukan adds 'love of God.'
Woe — love the best seat in synagogues and greetings in the marketplaces. Vanity-seeking.
Woe — you are like unmarked graves, and people walk over them without knowing it. Hidden defilement spreads ritual uncleanness unwittingly. Their hypocrisy contaminates without anyone seeing it.
Lawyer: 'Teacher, in saying these things you insult us also.' Self-identifying objection.
'Woe to you lawyers also! You load people with burdens hard to bear, and you yourselves do not touch the burdens with one of your fingers.' Burden-imposers without burden-bearing.
Woe — you build the tombs of the prophets, whom your fathers killed.
You are witnesses and consent to your fathers' deeds — they killed them, you build their tombs. Monument-building is endorsement, not memorial.
'The Wisdom of God said: I will send them prophets and apostles, some of whom they will kill and persecute.' Prophetic succession will be persecuted in turn.
Charged against this generation: all the blood of all prophets shed from the foundation of the world.
From Abel to Zechariah (killed between altar and sanctuary — 2 Chronicles 24). The accumulated guilt converges.
Woe — taken away the key of knowledge. You did not enter yourselves, and you hindered those who were entering. Gatekeepers blocking entry.
When he went outside, scribes and Pharisees began to press him hard and to provoke him to speak about many things.
Lying in wait for him, to catch something he might say. Hostility hardens — they now look for ammunition.
